watch_list.roast: EarthDaily launched six additional satellites in May 2026 and hit CEOS-ARD compliance in March, which means they are no longer a PowerPoint, they are an actual constellation with actual data that meets actual standards. They also acquired SkyForest for wildfire, Descartes Labs for insurance and mining, and EarthDaily Agro for agriculture, which means they now own domain expertise across five verticals instead of renting it. That is the riskiest and most capital-intensive path in EO, and it only works if Antarctica Capital keeps writing checks and the verticals they picked actually scale. Paul applied here in March as an Account Executive, which was probably the wrong level, but the timing is better now. They have the satellites, the data, the verticals, and presumably a growing pipeline of buyers who need someone to translate constellation capability into a commercial argument that makes sense to an insurance underwriter or a mining exploration manager. Paul has done that on both sides. At KSAT he sold to the super majors. At GFW he bought from Planet and structured the three-party deal that got it closed. If EarthDaily wants someone who can walk into a room with a commodities buyer and explain why six-satellite daily revisit is worth the price premium over Sentinel, he has run that negotiation and knows which objections are real and which ones are just the buyer practicing their poker face.

watch_list.why: Building weather and climate satellites. Commercial partnerships team would be a fit.
watch_list.roast: Named after a subatomic particle because 'Boring But Reliable Climate Satellite Company' doesn't fit on a business card — but that IS the pitch and in 2026 it's a winning one. While Starcloud raises $170M to put data centers in orbit and Windborne floats balloon confetti into the stratosphere, Muon just keeps launching working satellites that measure real things — temperature, water vapor, greenhouse gas plumes — and selling the data to NOAA, NASA, and the reinsurance majors who need to model hurricane seasons that keep breaking records. Real hardware, real contracts, engineers who've actually bent metal, in a sector full of PowerPoint constellations. watch_list.why: Bremen-based, first satellite launching 2026, VHR optical + AI analytics, European defense angle fits KSAT/GFW pattern perfectly
watch_list.roast: Bremen startup with five million euros and a launch date. You have spent a decade explaining to oil majors why they should pay for imagery they did not know they needed, then another five years explaining to foundations why a free platform should also have a commercial program. Marble is the inverse bet. They are building the constellation before the buyer fully exists, betting European defense ministries will panic-buy sovereign EO capacity after watching US companies turn off the feed during Iran. The thesis is clean. Sovereignty sells when the alternative is a voluntary withhold notice from Planet Labs. You know the KSAT playbook. Sell the Norwegians first because they will pay to not depend on Maxar. Then sell the narrative that proactive monitoring becomes required, not optional. Marble has the timing advantage because the Iran imagery blackout just reminded every EU defense minister that dependency is a liability. They need a Director of Partnerships who understands how to translate a satellite into a regulatory argument. The risk is they are too early or the EU moves too slowly, and five million does not last long in the sat game. But if you are looking for a role where the entire commercial function is zero to one, and the pitch is write the requirement before the RFP exists, you have done this twice already. The difference is you would be doing it in Germany instead of Tromso, and for defense instead of oil spill. Not a perfect fit, but the bones are right.

watch_list.why: Bangalore, first multi-sensor satellite (SAR + optical on one platform) launching Q1 2026, 160kg, targeting insurance/defense/ag
watch_list.roast: Indian startup launching the first satellite that puts SAR and optical on the same bus, which sounds like the kind of technical elegance that makes an engineer happy and a commercial buyer confused. You spent three years at GFW trying to explain why someone would pay for vessel detection data that a free platform already shows. GalaxEye has the opposite problem. They have a genuinely differentiated technical product and now they need to find the buyer who understands why multi-sensor fusion matters before the competitor launches a simpler single-sensor product at half the cost. The market is insurance, defense, ag, infrastructure, which is startup-speak for we will sell to whoever answers the phone. You know this game. The technical capability is never the constraint. The constraint is finding the three verticals where multi-sensor data creates a decision advantage worth the contract price, then building the partnerships that make the buyer trust you before the data exists. Bangalore puts you twelve hours ahead of Taos, which is either a feature or a disaster depending on how you feel about morning calls with the UK and evening calls with California. The company has traction, the tech is legitimately differentiated, and they are at the stage where the commercial function needs to be built with intention instead of hope. The question is whether you want to build the India EO market from scratch, or whether you would rather do the same work for a company that already has European or US distribution. If the answer is the former, this is the role. If the answer is the latter, keep looking.

watch_list.why: Berlin-based, ESA-backed, building Twinspector constellation for infrastructure monitoring, secured seven-figure ESA funding April 2026
watch_list.roast: German company that convinced ESA to write a seven-figure check to build a dedicated constellation for infrastructure monitoring, which means somebody in Europe finally decided that proactive utility monitoring is a business instead of a science project. You know the pattern. KSAT sold Shell on proactive offshore monitoring by proving the regulator would eventually require it. LiveEO is running the same play but for power lines and pipelines on land instead of rigs at sea. The difference is the utility sector moves slower than oil majors, which is saying something. They have traction, they have ESA backing, and they just closed funding in April 2026, which means they are probably hiring for the next phase right now. The commercial challenge is identical to what you faced at KSAT in 2013. The technical capability is proven. The buyer knows they need it eventually. The question is how do you make eventually become now. You do that by finding the utility that has the most exposure, running a paid pilot that creates a precedent, then using that precedent to move the regulator. Once the regulator moves, every other utility has to buy or risk looking negligent. You have run this play twice. You could run it again for LiveEO, but this time for European utilities instead of Australian offshore oil. The role would be building the commercial function and the regulatory strategy in parallel, which is exactly the kind of market-making work you do well. The downside is Berlin is not Taos, and the utility sector is slower than oil and gas, which you may have finally lost patience for. But if the idea of getting paid to move a regulator appeals to you, the bones are right.

watch_list.why: Israeli startup using satellite data, climate models, and AI to build resilience platforms for land-based supply chains (agriculture, forestry). Direct EO application, climate-adjacent, mission-commercial tension Paul knows well.
watch_list.roast: Marvin is an AI copilot for resilient agricultural supply chains, which sounds like someone fed a venture pitch generator the words satellite, climate, and value chain and hit enter three times. The company is Israeli, the domain is hard, and the customer is an agribusiness trying to figure out whether a vegetation index means replant or wait two weeks. You spent a decade at KSAT convincing oil majors that proactive monitoring beats reactive crisis management. Marvin is selling the same argument to a different vertical, except the regulator is slower, the margin is thinner, and the buyer does not know what threshold triggers action. They raised money, they have a product, and someone will eventually figure out what the copilot is supposed to do when the model says the harvest fails in June. The opportunity is proving that the platform can sell to more than one crop, more than one country, and more than one skeptical procurement officer who has seen five satellite startups pitch this year. If you can translate KSAT's multi-vertical playbook into agricultural risk management, this is the kind of builder-stage infrastructure company you could actually help scale. If not, it is a really nice slide deck about the future of food systems.

watch_list.why: Converts biomass into carbon-rich liquid, injects it underground for permanent removal. Durability-focused, scaling commercially, direct climate impact with verifiable MRV. Paul's GFW commercial-mission framing applies cleanly.
watch_list.roast: Charm Industrial takes agricultural waste, turns it into bio-oil, and pumps it underground where it stays for thousands of years. It is one of the few carbon removal pathways that does not require you to pretend enhanced weathering is ready for prime time or that direct air capture pencils out without a hyperscaler writing the check. They have actual removal credits sold, actual injection sites operating, and actual customers who care whether the carbon stays buried or leaks back out in fifteen years. You built the Planet deal at GFW by knowing which buyers pay for verified outcomes instead of aspirational offsets. Charm is the same bet in a different market. The carbon credit world is a wasteland of consultants, registries, and startups that cannot explain their methodology without a whiteboard and twenty minutes. Charm is one of the few that can say we put it in the ground, here is the well, here is the monitoring, here is the third party attestation. The commercial challenge is not the science. It is whether enterprises actually allocate budget to durable removal or whether they keep buying cheap forestry credits and calling it net zero. If Paul can help them frame the value proposition so the CFO and the chief sustainability officer both nod, they win. If not, it is another breakthrough technology waiting for the market to catch up.

watch_list.why: Building satellite and radar-based extreme weather forecasting systems for agriculture, logistics, energy. High-frequency EO + AI, enterprise customers, operational decision-making use case. Adjacent to Paul's GFW real-time data experience.
watch_list.roast: Climavision is trying to make weather forecasting fast enough and accurate enough that someone will actually change their logistics route, delay a harvest, or shut down a transmission line before the storm hits instead of after. The company runs its own weather radars, ingests satellite data, wraps it in AI-driven prediction models, and sells subscriptions to industries that lose money every time they guess wrong about a thunderstorm. The pitch is solid. The technical execution is real. The business model is recurring revenue from enterprises that already budget for weather intelligence. The problem is the same problem every high-frequency EO company eventually hits. Buyers believe the data matters right up until they have to integrate it into an actual operational workflow, at which point someone in the organization says we already have NOAA for free and this costs how much per seat. You sold KSAT oil spill monitoring into a market that had no category and no budget line. Climavision is selling into a market that has both but assumes the free version is good enough. If Paul can help them articulate why sub-hour forecasting is worth enterprise budget and how to structure a pilot that proves ROI in the first 90 days, they have a shot. If not, it is another weather company with better models and no path to the decision maker who actually signs.

watch_list.why: Dutch EO startup using passive microwave sensing for soil moisture, vegetation, and weather insights. Agriculture, insurance, banking, commodities verticals. Established European player, commercially mature buyer base. Paul's GFW insurance work (AXA Climate, British Marine/QBE) is directly adjacent, and the multi-sector licensing model echoes KSAT's multi-vertical services.
watch_list.roast: VanderSat sells microwave-derived soil moisture and vegetation data to agriculture, insurance, banking, and commodities buyers, which is a polite way of saying they sell the same dataset to four different industries and call it four different products depending on who is asking. They have been at this since 2014, so they are not a moonshot, they are a functioning commercial operation with actual recurring customers, which in the EO world makes them a statistical anomaly. The microwave angle is clever because it works through cloud cover, which means they can monitor crops in the Netherlands in February, which is otherwise impossible and also depressing. The challenge is that they are in the most competitive segment of EO. Every agtech platform, every precision agriculture startup, and half the insurance industry has a satellite data partnership. VanderSat has survived a decade, so they clearly have something defensible, but Paul would need to understand what it is. Is it the microwave sensor advantage? Is it the algorithm? Or is it just that they were early and the customers have no reason to switch? If it is the third one, that is fine, but it means the growth story is about adding new verticals, not expanding within existing ones. Paul has done that. GFW's insurance vertical was net-new, built from a use case the buyers did not know they had. If VanderSat wants to find the fifth vertical, he has run that play.

watch_list.why: UK startup building climate risk reporting tools using satellite EO data for biodiversity, drought, heat stress, wildfire. Targets businesses, legislators, environmentalists. Aligns with evolving environmental laws. Paul's GFW conservation mission, open-access commercial tension, and Planet partnership (monitoring use case creation) are all relevant. UK-based, small team, mission-commercial balance.
watch_list.roast: Map Impact sells climate risk reporting tools built on satellite data, which sounds like every ESG analytics pitch from the last four years except they actually name the tools BiodiversityView, DroughtView, HeatView, WildfireView, like they are releasing a Marvel franchise. They are a UK startup, probably six people in a co-working space in Shoreditch, and they are trying to turn satellite-derived risk scores into something a compliance officer will pay for annually. The opportunity is real. EUDR is in force, CSRD is rolling out, and every corporation with a supply chain in a forest is suddenly very interested in whether that forest still exists. The risk is that Map Impact is one layer removed from the actual pain. They do not sell to the companies that own the land, they sell to the companies that report on the companies that own the land. That is a viable business, but it is a services business dressed up as a data business, and the margins reflect it. Paul has spent his whole career one step closer to the operational buyer, the person who actually has to make a decision based on the data. If Map Impact wants to move upstream and sell directly to the asset owner or the supply chain manager, that is a different motion, and Paul has done it. GFW built the ASN subsea cable deal by identifying a non-obvious use case for vessel detection data and then constructing the commercial argument from scratch. Map Impact has the data and the regulatory tailwind. The question is whether they can build the argument that makes the buyer feel like they have no choice but to pay.

haiku:
Commercial from zero\
three times now, at three shops\
the map is the man

Shell, ENI, Exxon\
closed the first five the hard way\
the sixth was simpler

Twenty-five years in\
still building something from scratch\
still the best version

Telemark turns through\
late-spring corn on the south face\
worth the dawn patrol

Backpack on the trail\
no cell signal for three days\
best interview prep

Pecos in June\
thunder at three, camp by five\
summer's honest deal

A-Basin holds on\
June skiing at ten thousand\
stubborn like the search

Weminuche calls\
a week without a phone or pitch\
the answer gets clear

Tuckerman Ravine\
the last of April's big bowls\
worth a redeye flight

The CDT moves\
thru-hikers through Cumbres today\
summer is coming

Wind River high route\
ten days, one sat phone, no signal\
good for the soul math

The dashboard refreshes\
twelve o eight, twelve o eight daily\
rhythm holds the work

Morning brief arrives\
scores, blockers, one-line verdicts\
open, read, decide

An em dash appears\
the gate catches it and yells loud\
rewrite at the source

A hard requirement\
caps the score at forty-two points\
the rubric holds firm

CBAM price drops in\
seventy-five per the ton\
the steel mill writes checks

MethaneSAT is dark\
the billion-dollar eye died\
ghost in the pipeline

Taos under wind\
fire gate closes on the sage\
forty-five-mile gusts

Kachina is closed\
the snow left in early March\
mud season arrives

Merlin remaps Earth\
daily pass at one meter\
orbit holds the plan

Cormac stays in print\
Blood Meridian rereads\
judge in the desert

Abbey's ghost still haunts\
monkey wrench in the glovebox\
Hayduke would not file

Sangre de Cristo\
the peaks hold April's last white\
snow line creeps upward

Gorge bridge at high noon\
hawks ride wind above the scour\
six hundred feet down

Tromsø in April\
polar light returns to sea\
the dark was a friend

Forty-eight no thanks\
forty-ninth still has a pulse\
keep the net in play

Fire ban in the sage\
statewide, all the fuel too dry\
spring came in thirsty

EPA repeals\
endangerment finding gone\
the lawsuits begin

By decree it goes\
deep-sea mining by April\
the seabed gets sold

Tanager finds plumes\
ten methane leaks in the field\
California knocks

Cora packs his box\
ten and seventeen broke him\
Tracy gets the keys

Tracy in the chair\
five to three on debut night\
Contreras hits one

Lomu in round one\
Utah tackle plugs the line\
the trench gets adult

One percent snowpack\
Sangres east drained out by March\
the river runs thin

Palo Flechado\
four percent of average\
the ditches still wait

Acequias dry\
rationing in Talpa now\
fire damage still rules

Artemis returns\
four around the moon and home\
fifty years between

Ten deals took the cake\
twenty-eight percent of all\
late-stage eats the rest

Brussels punts again\
EUDR slides a year\
pilots get their breath

Mud splatters the truck\
April rolls into a May\
that looks like July

Cast wider, he said\
the net pulls in stranger fish\
breadth before the bite

Corn snow at sunrise\
spring lines hold above tree line\
dawn patrol still pays

Viasat goes up\
Falcon Heavy on the Cape\
twenty-seventh dusk

Secret Service swept
the dinner, but the inbox stays
quiet in Taos sun

Sox won on Patriots Day
eight runs, zero homers, grit
Cora shrugs, moves on

Svalbard ten days warm
above zero in April
KSAT saw it first

Planet shuts the feed
government asks, image stops
open data dies

G7 in Paris
climate left off the agenda
to keep Trump at table

Red Willow Park trail
closed one day this week for work
the gorge never closes

Correspondents fled
bulletproof vest held the line
haiku holds the shift

Food systems at brink
UN warns, heat redefines yield
mesa dust agrees

ATS black hole swallows
five tailored cover letters
haiku make it through

The wire stays quiet
April mud, April waiting
Wheeler holds the snow

Redhead at the bar
asked what I do for living
satellite silence

Sox fewest homers
American League basement
still beat the Tigers

Strait of Hormuz closed
oil at ninety, Sox at nine
each leak costs something

Trump scrapped Pakistan
Iran talks move to the phone
too much time wasted

Gas four eleven
fifth straight day climbing the wire
fossils still have teeth

Sub twenty minutes
the new tactical standard
BlackSky Gen Three lives

Sox won Marathon
Monday, eight to six, traffic
Narvaez stole third slow

Nine and sixteen now
small ball, no power, walks, grit
Yankees arrive Tuesday

Red Willow trail closed
April twenty seventh, Taos
temporary, spring work

Kachina shut March
twenty ninth, warm winter, base
seventeen inches gone

Late April haiku
the wire is quiet, sage blooms
gorge holds the long light

Monsoon two months out
mud season ends, Wheeler dries
Taos in between
